---
title: "05 · Rank, Linear Mappings, Change of Basis"
subtitle: "The dimension of a matrix's column space is the number of independent things it can do — and that number is invariant under change of basis."
lesson-id: "mml.rank-and-linear-mappings"
tier: "mml"
prerequisites: ["mml.linear-independence-basis"]
unlocks: ["mml.affine-spaces"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you state what a basis is and why every basis of $\mathbb{R}^n$
   has size $n$? — see
   [MML — 04, Linear Independence](../04-linear-independence-basis/index.qmd).
2. Can you compute $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$ as a linear
   combination of $\boldsymbol{X}$'s columns? — see
   [MML — 02, Matrices](../02-matrices/index.qmd).
3. Do you know what the column space of $\boldsymbol{X}$ is? —
   [MML — 03, Vector Spaces](../03-vector-spaces/index.qmd).
4. Can you state the determinant of a $2 \times 2$ matrix?

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

Drag the four entries of a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$. The
widget shows what happens to a *unit square* $[0,1] \times [0,1]$
under the linear map $\boldsymbol{x} \mapsto \boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x}$.

```{ojs}
viewof a11 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value:  1, step: 0.1, label: "a₁₁:"})
viewof a12 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value:  0, step: 0.1, label: "a₁₂:"})
viewof a21 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value:  0, step: 0.1, label: "a₂₁:"})
viewof a22 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value:  1, step: 0.1, label: "a₂₂:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  function ap(p) { return [a11*p[0] + a12*p[1], a21*p[0] + a22*p[1]]; }
  const corners = [[0,0],[1,0],[1,1],[0,1],[0,0]];
  const mapped = corners.map(ap);
  const det = a11*a22 - a12*a21;
  const rank = (Math.abs(det) > 1e-7) ? 2 :
               ((Math.abs(a11)+Math.abs(a12)+Math.abs(a21)+Math.abs(a22) > 1e-7) ? 1 : 0);
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return html`<div>
    <div style="font:13px sans-serif; margin-bottom:4px;">rank(A) = <b>${rank}</b>,  |det A| = ${Math.abs(det).toFixed(2)}  ⇒ image is ${
      rank===2 ? "a parallelogram (full 2D)" :
      rank===1 ? "a line segment (rank-1, collapsed)" :
                 "a single point (rank-0)"
    }</div>
    ${Plot.plot({
      width: 460, height: 360,
      x: {domain: [-5, 5], label: "x"},
      y: {domain: [-5, 5], label: "y"},
      grid: true,
      marks: [
        Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#bbb"}),
        Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#bbb"}),
        Plot.line(corners.map(p => ({x:p[0], y:p[1]})), {x:"x", y:"y", stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "3 3"}),
        Plot.line(mapped.map(p => ({x:p[0], y:p[1]})), {x:"x", y:"y", stroke: "steelblue", strokeWidth: 2, fill: "rgba(70,130,180,0.15)"})
      ]
    })}
  </div>`;
}
```

When the matrix is *full rank* (det ≠ 0), the unit square is mapped
to a parallelogram. When the matrix is *rank 1*, the square collapses
to a *line segment* — one entire dimension was destroyed by the map.
When rank 0 (the zero matrix), everything goes to a single point.

## Wonder

1. Why does the *area* of the image parallelogram equal $|\det A|$?
   What would happen in 3D — would it be a volume?
2. A rank-1 map sends every input to a *line*. Can such a map ever
   "undo" itself — i.e. be inverted?
3. The standard basis $\boldsymbol{e}_1, \boldsymbol{e}_2$ goes to
   the *columns* of $\boldsymbol{A}$. Is that a coincidence?
4. If we used a different basis for $\mathbb{R}^2$, would the same
   linear map have a different matrix? Would its rank change?

## Discover

Six problems building rank, linear maps, and change of basis.

**D1.** Let
$\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$.
What is the rank of $\boldsymbol{A}$ (dimension of its column space)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The column space is $\mathrm{span}\{(1,4), (2,5), (3,6)\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$.
Any two non-parallel columns span $\mathbb{R}^2$. Here $(1, 4)$ and
$(2, 5)$ are independent ($1 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 4 = -3 \ne 0$).
Three columns can't all be independent in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Rank = 2.
</details>

**D2.** Let $\boldsymbol{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$.
What is rank $\boldsymbol{B}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Column 2 = $2 \times$ Column 1 — dependent. Rank = 1. The column
space is the line through $(1, 2, 3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
</details>

**D3.** Define $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ by $T(x, y) = (x + y, x - y)$.
Verify $T(\alpha \boldsymbol{u} + \beta \boldsymbol{v}) = \alpha T(\boldsymbol{u}) + \beta T(\boldsymbol{v})$ —
i.e. that $T$ is **linear**. Find the matrix of $T$ in the standard
basis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The $i$-th column of the matrix is $T(\boldsymbol{e}_i)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$T(\boldsymbol{e}_1) = T(1, 0) = (1, 1)$,
$T(\boldsymbol{e}_2) = T(0, 1) = (1, -1)$.
Matrix: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$. Linearity:
$T(\alpha \boldsymbol{u} + \beta \boldsymbol{v}) = ((\alpha u_1 + \beta v_1) + (\alpha u_2 + \beta v_2), (\alpha u_1 + \beta v_1) - (\alpha u_2 + \beta v_2)) = \alpha T(\boldsymbol{u}) + \beta T(\boldsymbol{v})$.
</details>

**D4.** Take the same map $T$. Express its action in the *new basis*
$\boldsymbol{b}_1 = (1, 1), \boldsymbol{b}_2 = (1, -1)$ —
i.e. find a matrix $\boldsymbol{A}'$ such that the coordinates of
$T(\boldsymbol{v})$ in the new basis are $\boldsymbol{A}'$ times the
coordinates of $\boldsymbol{v}$ in the new basis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply $T$ to $\boldsymbol{b}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{b}_2$, then express
the results back in the basis $\{\boldsymbol{b}_1, \boldsymbol{b}_2\}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$T(\boldsymbol{b}_1) = T(1, 1) = (2, 0) = 1 \cdot (1, 1) + 1 \cdot (1, -1) = \boldsymbol{b}_1 + \boldsymbol{b}_2$. So the first column of $\boldsymbol{A}'$ is $(1, 1)^\top$ — *in $\boldsymbol{b}$-coordinates*.
$T(\boldsymbol{b}_2) = T(1, -1) = (0, 2) = 1 \cdot (1, 1) - 1 \cdot (1, -1) = \boldsymbol{b}_1 - \boldsymbol{b}_2$. Second column: $(1, -1)^\top$.
So $\boldsymbol{A}' = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ — same matrix! (Because the basis we chose happens to be related to $T$ symmetrically.) In general $\boldsymbol{A}'$ and $\boldsymbol{A}$ will differ.
</details>

**D5.** For a general change of basis with matrix $\boldsymbol{S}$
(columns = new basis vectors in old coordinates), the new matrix of
$T$ is $\boldsymbol{A}' = \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$.
Verify by checking the formula on the previous problem.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{S} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{S}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.
$\boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} = \tfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \tfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \boldsymbol{I}$.
$\boldsymbol{A}' = \boldsymbol{I} \boldsymbol{S} = \boldsymbol{S} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.
Matches D4. The formula $\boldsymbol{A}' = \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$ is *change of basis* in matrix form.
</details>

**D6.** Rank doesn't change under change of basis. Why? (Informally.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiplication by an *invertible* matrix doesn't shrink or grow the
column space — it just re-labels it.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The column space of $\boldsymbol{A}' = \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$
is the image of $\boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A}$ — and since
$\boldsymbol{S}^{-1}$ is invertible, it preserves dimension. So
$\mathrm{rank}(\boldsymbol{A}') = \mathrm{rank}(\boldsymbol{A})$.
Rank is an **invariant** of the linear map — independent of which
basis you write it in.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The word "rank" was first used in this sense by **Frobenius** in 1879.
But "linear mapping" as a stand-alone idea took until **Peano (1888)** to
crystallise. Before that, mathematicians thought in terms of "linear
substitutions" — transformations applied to systems of equations — which
is why a lot of 19th-century linear algebra reads like notation about
notation. Recognising the *operator* as the object of study (independent
of its matrix) is one of the deepest moves in abstract mathematics.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A matrix is a **linear map written in a chosen basis**. Change the basis
and the matrix changes — but its *rank*, *determinant*, *eigenvalues*,
*trace*, and *nullity* don't. Those are properties of the **mapping**,
not the matrix. Most of ML's linear algebra is about choosing the right
basis to make a hard mapping look easy.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}
A **linear map** between vector spaces $V$ and $W$ is a function
$\Phi : V \to W$ satisfying
$$
\Phi(\alpha \boldsymbol{u} + \beta \boldsymbol{v}) = \alpha \Phi(\boldsymbol{u}) + \beta \Phi(\boldsymbol{v})
\quad \text{for all } \boldsymbol{u}, \boldsymbol{v} \in V,\ \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}.
$$

Once you choose a basis of $V$ and a basis of $W$, the map $\Phi$ is
represented by a unique **matrix** whose $i$-th column is $\Phi$ applied
to the $i$-th basis vector of $V$, written in the chosen basis of $W$.

The **rank** of a matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$ is the dimension of its
column space — equivalently, the number of linearly independent
columns. (And, by a non-trivial theorem, equivalently the number of
linearly independent rows.)

Under change of basis $\boldsymbol{S}$, a matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$
representing a map $V \to V$ transforms to
$\boldsymbol{A}' = \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$.
This operation is called **conjugation** by $\boldsymbol{S}$.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Sūtra.** *The map is the object; the matrix is a name; the basis is
the dictionary.*
:::
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
**Watch out:**

1. *"A matrix and its corresponding linear map are the same thing."* —
   Not exactly. The map is intrinsic; the matrix depends on a chosen
   basis. Two different matrices can represent the same map.
2. *"Rank counts the number of non-zero rows."* — Almost — but only
   after row-reduction (Gaussian elimination). The raw count of
   non-zero rows in an unreduced matrix is not the rank.
3. *"Linear maps are matrices in disguise."* — In finite dimensions,
   essentially yes. In infinite dimensions (function spaces), most
   linear maps cannot be written as matrices — they are
   **operators**.
:::

## Apply

### Worked example

A regression with three highly correlated features:
$\boldsymbol{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \\ 4 & 8 & 12 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 3}$.
What goes wrong if we try to solve the normal equations
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{w} = \boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$?

**Step 1.** Observe the columns: $\boldsymbol{x}_2 = 2 \boldsymbol{x}_1$,
$\boldsymbol{x}_3 = 3 \boldsymbol{x}_1$. Rank of $\boldsymbol{X}$ is 1.

**Step 2.** $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$.
Its rank is the same as $\boldsymbol{X}$'s rank (a theorem): 1. So
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ is *singular* — its determinant
is $0$, and it has no inverse.

**Step 3.** The normal equations have either no solution or infinitely
many. If a solution exists, every parameter $\boldsymbol{w}$ along
the null space of $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ gives the
same predictions $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$. *The model is not
identifiable.*

**Conclusion:** Rank-deficient design matrices break linear regression.
The fix is either (a) drop redundant features, (b) add a small
$\lambda \boldsymbol{I}$ to restore independence (ridge regression),
or (c) project onto the column space (PCA pre-processing).

### Warm

**W1.** What is the rank of $\boldsymbol{I}_n$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$n$ — every standard basis column is independent.
</details>

**W2.** What is the rank of the zero matrix?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
0 — no non-zero columns.
</details>

**W3.** A $3 \times 5$ matrix can have rank at most what?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\min(3, 5) = 3$. Rank can't exceed either dimension. (3 rows × 5
columns: column space lives in $\mathbb{R}^3$, so dimension at most 3.)
</details>

### Working

**Wo1.** Show that the kernel (= null space)
$\ker \Phi = \{\boldsymbol{v} : \Phi(\boldsymbol{v}) = \boldsymbol{0}\}$
of a linear map is always a subspace of the domain.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{0} \in \ker \Phi$ (linearity).
$\boldsymbol{u}, \boldsymbol{v} \in \ker \Phi \Rightarrow \Phi(\boldsymbol{u} + \boldsymbol{v}) = \boldsymbol{0} + \boldsymbol{0} = \boldsymbol{0}$.
$\Phi(\lambda \boldsymbol{u}) = \lambda \boldsymbol{0} = \boldsymbol{0}$. Three subspace checks pass.
</details>

**Wo2.** State the **rank-nullity theorem**:
$\dim V = \mathrm{rank}\,\boldsymbol{A} + \mathrm{nullity}\,\boldsymbol{A}$,
where $\boldsymbol{A}$ represents a map from $V$. Interpret it in
plain English.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The dimension of the domain equals the dimension you "use" (rank,
i.e. column-space dimension) plus the dimension you "destroy"
(nullity, the dimension of the kernel). Every input direction either
goes somewhere or gets crushed — and the total is fixed. For
$\boldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $\mathrm{rank} + \mathrm{nullity} = n$.
</details>

**Wo3.** Give the matrix of the linear map $\mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$
that rotates by $90°$ about the $z$-axis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(1, 0, 0) \mapsto (0, 1, 0)$, $(0, 1, 0) \mapsto (-1, 0, 0)$,
$(0, 0, 1) \mapsto (0, 0, 1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{R} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Rank = 3 (full), determinant = 1, columns are the
images of the basis vectors. We'll see rotations again in Lesson 09.
</details>

### Stretch

**S1.** A convolutional layer in a neural network applies a fixed
$k \times k$ filter to every position of an input image. Is this
operation linear? What's the "matrix" of this operation, viewed as a
map from images to images?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes, convolution is linear: $\text{conv}(\alpha \boldsymbol{x} + \beta \boldsymbol{y}) = \alpha \,\text{conv}(\boldsymbol{x}) + \beta \,\text{conv}(\boldsymbol{y})$. The matrix is a **Toeplitz** matrix — sparse and structured,
with the filter weights repeating along diagonals. Modern frameworks
*never* materialise this matrix (too large), but it exists conceptually.
The fact that conv is linear is what makes it work with all of linear
algebra's tools (backprop, eigenanalysis, etc.).
</details>

**S2.** PCA computes a change of basis. In the new basis, the
*covariance matrix* of the data is diagonal. Why might that simplify
subsequent analyses?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Diagonal covariance means the features are uncorrelated. Any
component-wise analysis (per-feature regularisation, per-feature
analysis of variance, marginal distributions) decouples completely.
Many algorithms simplify dramatically (independent Gaussians, separable
priors, parallelisable updates) when features are uncorrelated. We'll
prove PCA does this in Lesson 26.
</details>

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A matrix is one *name* for a linear map; the map is what's real, and rank is its invariant size.**
2. **Rank-nullity: every input dimension is either *used* (rank) or *crushed* (nullity); the sum is the domain dimension.**
3. **In ML, low rank is a data structure: redundant features collapse, and the right basis often makes the geometry trivial (this is why PCA, SVD, and embeddings matter).**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Can you state, without notes, what changes under change of basis
   and what stays the same?
2. Did you solve at least 6 of the 9 practice problems without
   peeking at hints?
3. Can you reach for "rank = number of independent directions" as a
   first instinct when reading any matrix-heavy paper?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 06.
- **2 yes:** redo D5 and Wo2, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* problems D4–D6.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`mml.linear-independence-basis`](../04-linear-independence-basis/index.qmd) —
  rank is the size of a basis of the column space; without "basis,"
  rank has no meaning.

### What this unlocks

- [`mml.affine-spaces`](../06-affine-spaces/index.qmd) — affine maps
  are linear maps plus a translation; same rank language applies.
- Eventually, [`mml.svd-and-low-rank`](../13-svd-and-low-rank/index.qmd) —
  SVD gives a canonical "best rank-$k$" approximation to any matrix.

### Sister synthesis lesson

[`t5.matrices`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd)
introduces rank via row-reduction; this lesson treats it as the
**dimension of the map's image** — the conceptual definition that
makes rank-nullity, SVD, and PCA inevitable.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Multicollinearity = rank-deficient design matrix. Ridge
regression = add $\lambda \boldsymbol{I}$ to lift rank deficiency.
Low-rank approximation (Lesson 13) = the matrix-compression idea
behind LoRA fine-tuning of large language models.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Query optimisation cost models use rank-of-join-graph
heuristics. A low-rank join hint can short-circuit expensive plans.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** "Effective dimensionality" of a dataset is rarely the
ambient dimension — natural data lives on a low-dimensional manifold
inside $\mathbb{R}^D$. The first cut is "estimate the rank of the
covariance matrix," which is exactly what PCA does.
:::
